configurations.name: with_skill
configurations.label: Improved Skills (multi-severity guidance + CVE disambiguation + stale-data)

observations: Improved skills score 14/14 (100%) vs old skills 11/14 (78.6%) — a +21.4% improvement.
Three assertions discriminate between old and new skills:

- single_call_omitting_severity: Old skills lead to two separate API calls (one per severity). While this works in simulation, the production LLM (Gemini) may instead attempt severity=['Critical','Important'] which causes the MCP tool error observed in production logs.
- cites_multi_severity_strategy: The new multi-severity section in tool-invocation-rules is explicitly cited, showing the LLM is following documented guidance rather than inferring correct behavior.
- flags_stale_checkins: The new skills activate guardrails-safety for system listings and flag check-ins older than 24 hours. The old skills did not activate guardrails-safety for this query type.
Eval 5 (advisor-recommendations) is non-discriminating: both versions handle advisor queries correctly. This serves as a regression test.
non_discriminating_assertions: uses_get_cves_not_system_cves — Both versions correctly pick the global CVE tool
uses_affecting_filter — Both correctly scope to affecting CVEs
uses_cvss_sort — Both sort by CVSS score
uses_advisor_tools — Both correctly pick advisor tools
proper_advisor_args — Both use correct parameter types
qualitative_differences: Eval 4: with_skill uses 1 API call vs old_skill's 2 calls — more efficient and eliminates the risk of passing severity as a list.
Eval 4: with_skill explicitly names the failure mode ('we must NOT pass severity=["Critical","Important"]') which matches the exact production error.
Eval 6: with_skill flags 3 edge nodes with stale data (>17 hours) while old_skill silently presents them without warning.
The new CVE disambiguation table in multi-step-workflows gives the LLM explicit tool selection criteria for ambiguous CVE queries, reducing reliance on inference.
